# Path segments

::: callout-tip
## Objective

Return a different-length result for each url by building a list of character vectors.

:::

A url's path is split by slashes into **segments**. The url `https://example.com/foo/bar` has segments `foo` and `bar`. Each url has a different number of segments, so a flat character vector won't do. Instead we return a list where each element is a character vector of that url's segments.

## `path_segments()` returns an `Option`

The `path_segments()` method gives back `Option<Split>`. The `Split` is an iterator over the segments. It returns `None` for urls that "cannot be a base" (like `data:text/plain,...`), which have no path segments at all.

So we match on the `Option`:

- `Some(segments)` means we have an iterator. Collect the segments into a `Strings`.
- `None` means there are no segments. Return `NULL`.

## Exercise

- Write `url_path_segments(url: List) -> List`
- For each url, get the pointer with `get_extptr`
- Match on `path_segments()`
  - `Some(segments)`: collect the segments into a `Strings`
  - `None`: return `NULL`
- A failed pointer also returns `NULL`
- Collect into a `List`

<details>
    <summary>View solution</summary>

```rust
#[extendr]
fn url_path_segments(url: List) -> List {
    url.into_iter()
        .map(|(_, robj)| match RUrl::get_extptr(robj) {
            Ok(v) => match v.0.path_segments() {
                Some(segments) => segments
                    .map(|vi| Rstr::from(vi))
                    .collect::<Strings>()
                    .into_robj(),
                None => ().into_robj(),
            },
            Err(_) => ().into_robj(),
        })
        .collect()
}
```

</details>
